text: office hours were scheduled at impossible times for working students.
the instructor's slides on attention mechanisms were the clearest i have seen.
i appreciated how the professor highlighted limitations of every model we studied.
the course encouraged a growth mindset and celebrated incremental progress.
i felt disengaged because most of the material was theory with no application.
the assignments pushed me to learn and the feedback was constructive and timely.
several lectures were canceled with little notice and never rescheduled.
career advice woven into lectures was thoughtful and genuinely useful.
the course gave clear guidance on when classical methods beat deep learning.
project rubrics were vague and graders interpreted them differently across teams.
grading was fair and the rubrics were transparent from day one.
carefully designed problem sets steadily built up my intuition for evaluation metrics.
slides were dense walls of text that the professor read directly to us.
i improved my python and scikit-learn skills more than i expected to.
the frequent reorganization of the syllabus made long-term planning impossible.
grading felt arbitrary and similar submissions received very different scores.
the professor was responsive to feedback and adjusted the workload mid-semester.
lab equipment was unreliable and many sessions lost time to technical issues.
many lectures were essentially the same slides reused from previous semesters.
the midterm review session covered exactly the right material at the right depth.
project topics were restricted in ways that discouraged creativity.
this was one of the best electives i have taken at the university.
i appreciated how reproducibility was emphasized throughout the labs.
the reading list was curated thoughtfully and balanced theory with practical papers.
course assignments were aligned tightly with the learning outcomes.
i learned how to write a research-style report thanks to the project structure.
lab sessions were well structured and the starter notebooks saved a lot of setup time.
promised office hour expansions never materialized despite repeated requests.
live coding sessions made abstract algorithms feel concrete and approachable.
quizzes tested obscure trivia rather than the core ideas of the course.
teaching assistants graded promptly and provided very actionable comments.
my questions on transformers received answers that contradicted the course slides.
overall the course did not live up to what the syllabus had promised.
slide animations actively obscured rather than clarified the diagrams beneath them.
topics from earlier weeks were rarely revisited which made the exam stressful.
assignments were unclear and the grading rubric kept changing mid-stream.
i expected hands-on work with transformers but we only saw a single demo.
half the lectures consisted of the instructor reading slides verbatim without insight.
the error analysis exercise taught me to read confusion matrices critically.
the course relied too much on memorization rather than understanding.
